prompt: I'm writing a Go package that implements a custom line-based protocol (think Redis RESP but simplified). I need to test the parser. My colleague says I should create a MockConn struct that wraps a bytes.Buffer and implements net.Conn. Is that the right approach?
expected_skill_elements: Reject the bytes.Buffer mock approach explicitly
Explain why bytes.Buffer cannot reproduce TCP semantics (partial reads, blocking, connection close)
Provide or describe the testConn helper pattern using net.Listen('tcp', '127.0.0.1:0') and net.Dial
Note that port 0 assigns an unused port — no hardcoded ports
Show how to use both ends of the loopback pair in the test
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Suggesting bytes.Buffer, strings.NewReader, or io.Pipe as a net.Conn substitute
Recommending a mock framework to generate a MockConn
Suggesting the test only needs bytes.Buffer because 'it's just unit testing'
Omitting the goroutine pattern for Accept

expected_skill_elements: Identify the mockConn as the problem — it cannot reproduce TCP connection semantics
Name specific failure modes: partial reads return all bytes at once from Buffer, blocking reads return EOF on empty buffer instead of blocking, connection close is a no-op
Replace with testConn helper using net.Listen + net.Dial
Show revised test structure with real loopback pair
Mention port 0 and the goroutine for Accept
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Approving the mockConn approach
Only suggesting minor improvements to the mock (e.g. 'add SetDeadline behavior')
Suggesting io.Pipe as an improvement — it is better than bytes.Buffer but still not TCP
Not providing the concrete replacement

prompt: Write a testConn helper function in Go that I can use in my tests to get a real TCP connection pair. I want both the client and server ends returned so I can test my protocol code. Show me how to use it in a test too.
expected_skill_elements: net.Listen('tcp', '127.0.0.1:0') — loopback with OS-assigned port
Goroutine calling ln.Accept()
ln.Close() after accepting one connection
net.Dial connecting to ln.Addr().String()
Function signature: func testConn(t *testing.T) (net.Conn, net.Conn)
t.Fatalf (not error return) on any failure
Usage example showing defer client.Close() and defer server.Close()
Usage example writing to one end and reading from the other
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Returning an error from testConn instead of calling t.Fatalf
Hardcoding a port number instead of using :0
Not using a goroutine for Accept (which would deadlock)
Missing the ln.Close() after Accept
Not showing usage in a test

prompt: I have an HTTP handler I want to unit test. Should I use the testConn pattern with net.Listen and net.Dial?
expected_skill_elements: No — for HTTP, use net/http/httptest.NewServer() or httptest.NewRecorder()
Explain that testConn is for net.Conn (TCP/protocol level) code specifically
httptest is the standard library's equivalent for HTTP
Brief explanation of why: HTTP has its own higher-level test infrastructure
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Recommending testConn for HTTP handlers
Not mentioning net/http/httptest at all
Suggesting mockConn or bytes.Buffer for HTTP testing

prompt: I'm testing a gRPC service. Can I use the loopback testConn pattern?
expected_skill_elements: The loopback principle applies but gRPC has its own dedicated in-memory connection helper
Reference google.golang.org/grpc/test/bufconn as the gRPC-specific tool
Explain that bufconn is the gRPC equivalent — real in-memory connection, not a mock
testConn works at the raw net.Conn level; gRPC has its own abstraction layer
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Saying testConn is the right tool for gRPC without mentioning bufconn
Recommending mocking the gRPC client/server interface
Ignoring the gRPC-specific tooling entirely

prompt: net.Conn is a Go interface. Why can't I just mock it with a struct that has the right methods? I do this for other interfaces all the time.
expected_skill_elements: Acknowledge the technical point: you can implement the interface syntactically
Explain what the mock cannot reproduce: TCP partial reads, blocking on empty, connection-close error propagation, half-close
The mock tests the mock, not the protocol code
A real loopback pair is ~10 lines — the cost of 'just mock it' is not saved, it is spent on an inadequate substitute
Port 0 and the goroutine pattern make the real thing just as easy
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Agreeing that mocking is fine here
Saying io.Pipe is equivalent to a TCP connection
Not explaining the specific TCP semantics that are lost in a mock
Overstating the cost of the real loopback approach

prompt: How does HashiCorp's Packer test SSH connection handling? Walk me through the approach.
expected_skill_elements: Real in-process SSH server — not a mock
net.Listen on loopback, goroutine running SSH server handshake on accepted connection
Test dials with real SSH client
Both ends of the authenticated session returned to the test
Tests actual protocol behavior: key exchange, authentication, channel negotiation
Contrast with what a mock would test (nothing real about the protocol)
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Describing a mock SSH connection
Suggesting a pre-recorded bytes.Buffer response
Not connecting the Packer case to the general testConn principle

prompt: What is HashiCorp's policy on mocking net.Conn in tests?
expected_skill_elements: Direct quote or close paraphrase: 'There is no reason to ever mock net.Conn'
The policy applies across all HashiCorp projects
The alternative is the loopback TCP pair helper (~10 lines)
The reasoning: real TCP tests real behavior; mocks test the mock
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Softening the policy ('it depends')
Not providing the loopback alternative
Attributing the policy to something other than Hashimoto/HashiCorp

prompt: My protocol test passes locally but fails in CI with a race detector error. I'm using a bytes.Buffer mock for net.Conn. The mock's Read method is called from two goroutines simultaneously. How do I fix this?
expected_skill_elements: Root cause: the bytes.Buffer mock is not goroutine-safe AND is not a real TCP connection
The fix is not to add a mutex to the mock — the fix is to use a real loopback connection
net.Conn implementations from net.Listen/net.Dial are goroutine-safe by the Go net package guarantee
Provide or describe the testConn helper
The race detector error is a symptom of the deeper problem: using a mock instead of the real thing
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Suggesting adding a sync.Mutex to the mock as the solution
Not replacing the mock with a real loopback
Saying 'just protect the buffer with a mutex and you're done'

prompt: I have a TCP server that accepts connections and speaks a custom binary protocol. How do I write a unit test for the server's connection handler without starting a full server?
expected_skill_elements: testConn helper provides a real TCP pair without starting a full server
Pass the server-side connection end directly to the handler function under test
Write the protocol bytes from the client-side connection end
net.Listen('tcp', '127.0.0.1:0') — OS assigns port
t.Fatalf on errors, not error returns from the helper
defer both connections' Close()
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Starting a full server (net.Listen in the main test, not in the helper)
Using bytes.Buffer or io.Pipe
Hardcoding a port
Returning an error from the helper
